terms.definition: A named, reusable block of code defined with def that takes inputs and returns a value (None if no return is given).
A name listed in a function definition that stands for an input the function expects.
An actual value passed to a function when it is called, bound to the corresponding parameter.
An argument passed by name (e.g. temperature=0.2), making the call explicit and order-independent.
A fallback value used when an argument is omitted. Defaults are evaluated once at definition time, so mutable defaults should be avoided.
Catch-all parameters: *args collects extra positional arguments into a tuple, and **kwargs collects extra keyword arguments into a dict.
The region where a name is visible. Python resolves names Local, then Enclosing, then Global, then Built-in.
Declares that assignments to a name inside a function should rebind the module-level (global) variable rather than create a local one.
Declares that assignments to a name should rebind the variable in the nearest enclosing function, not create a new local one.
A nested function that captures and remembers variables from the enclosing scope where it was defined, even after that scope has returned.
A function whose output depends only on its inputs and which produces no side effects, making it predictable and easy to test.
